SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper reports experiments on FactBank with 5-fold cross-validation, macro/micro F1 metrics and a two-sample t-test (Section 5.1), and shows input ablations (Figure 6) supporting the role of SIP and auxiliary words. However, the provided excerpt does not include complete quantitative results for the main baseline comparisons, and the framework depends on upstream basic-factor extraction (Section 3) whose accuracy and error propagation are not reported. These omissions create some uncertainty about whether the empirical claims are fully supported and leave logical gaps about robustness to extraction errors.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper applies a combination of established neural components (BiLSTM, CNN and attention) to event factuality and introduces a two-step pipeline with an attention-based CNN for SIP detection (contributions 1–3). While applying these networks to factuality (and emphasizing SIP-path inputs) is a useful domain application, the core architectures and attention mechanisms are standard rather than novel methodological contributions. The paper therefore represents a moderate, task-focused novelty rather than a fundamentally new method.

CLARITY.justification: Overall organization follows a standard flow (introduction, factors, baseline, neural framework, experiments) and the main contributions are listed early (end of Section 1). However, the excerpt contains repeated/garbled text, LaTeX artifacts and some presentation issues which impede readability, and certain implementation details (e.g., exact training regimes, some input-extraction specifics) are described only briefly. Notation for the model is mostly defined (LSTM equations, attention) but the writing quality inconsistencies reduce clarity.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The experimental setup uses a real dataset (FactBank), multiple baselines (Rules, MaxEnt, CNN+CNN) and input ablation studies (Figure 6), which provide substantive evaluation of input contributions. Nevertheless, the paper lacks full numerical results in the provided excerpt, does not report performance of the upstream basic-factor extraction components, and provides little analysis of failure cases or limitations, which limits the depth and reproducibility of the study.

IMPACT.justification: Event factuality is an important NLP problem with relevance to downstream tasks (Introduction cites QA, textual entailment, rumor identification), so improvements are useful to the community. The contribution appears to be an incremental improvement (applying neural architectures and emphasizing SIP paths) rather than a transformative advance, so its impact is moderate: likely of interest to researchers working on factuality and related pipelines but not field-changing.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: The topic (event factuality identification) and methodology (neural sequence models, structured-syntactic inputs) are squarely within the scope of top NLP/ML venues. The paper uses standard datasets (FactBank), metrics (precision/recall/F1, macro/micro averaging) and competitive baselines appropriate for the audience, making it a good fit for such conferences.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The authors compare against a strong rule-based baseline (Saurí 2008), a MaxEnt feature-based system (de Marneffe et al. 2012) and a neural variant (CNN+CNN), and use the same FactBank data and evaluation metrics (Section 5.1). However, the excerpt omits full quantitative baseline numbers and does not report upstream extraction accuracies; comparisons to more recent neural approaches are limited, so while comparisons are generally fair, some relevant baselines and reporting details are missing.
